title: The case for incomplete archives
summary: Argue that archives and collections that preserve gaps, losses, and silences can be more honest — and more useful — than complete ones.
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) arguing that incomplete archives — collections with gaps, missing records, or deliberate silences — can be more honest and more intellectually useful than comprehensive ones. Consider what gets lost when institutions pursue completeness at all costs: whose voices are erased by the drive to catalogue, who decides what counts as worth preserving, and what an acknowledged gap communicates that a seamless collection cannot. Use a cleft sentence, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and a participle clause. Include at least one concrete case (a colonial archive, a censored state collection, a community memory project). Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What a gap in an archive records is not absence but the shape of a decision — a decision about…
Granted, a collection riddled with holes is harder to work with than a well-ordered one…
Having been assembled by colonial administrators rather than by the communities it describes…

hintVocab.meaningVi: kho lưu trữ / kho tài liệu lịch sử
khoảng trống / sự thiếu hụt trong tài liệu
lập danh mục / ghi vào kho lưu trữ
bị đàn áp / bị che giấu
bạo lực nhận thức luận — xóa bỏ hoặc bóp méo kiến thức của ai đó
kho lưu trữ thuộc địa
xóa bỏ / làm mờ
chọn lựa và tổ chức (bộ sưu tập, triển lãm)
nỗi ám ảnh về việc lưu trữ / mong muốn lưu giữ mọi thứ (thuật ngữ của Derrida)
bị giữ lại / không được công bố
xuất xứ / nguồn gốc của tài liệu
thừa nhận một khoảng trống
ký ức tổ chức / kiến thức được lưu giữ trong tổ chức

hintVocab.phrase: to be deemed worthy of preservation
hintVocab.meaningVi: được coi là xứng đáng được bảo tồn
truyền thống truyền miệng
sự im lặng của kho lưu trữ — những gì không được ghi lại
coi sự đầy đủ như một mục tiêu tuyệt đối
phi thuộc địa hóa (kho lưu trữ)
thiên lệch lựa chọn
bị làm cho vô hình
quyền lực của người lưu trữ
kho lưu trữ phản kháng — ghi lại những gì bị các tổ chức chính thống bỏ qua

sampleText:
What a gap in an archive records is not absence but the shape of a decision — a decision about whose experience was considered worth preserving and whose could be discarded without consequence. When the British colonial administration of Kenya destroyed thousands of documents before independence in 1963, the resulting lacunae in the official record were not accidental losses. They were acts. And an archivist working with those gaps, knowing what was destroyed and why, learns more from them than from any seamlessly complete collection.

The pursuit of completeness in archives is often presented as a neutral, professional ideal. In practice, it is anything but. Every archive is the product of choices: what to collect, what to classify, what to store, and — most consequentially — what to leave out. Having been assembled by colonial administrators rather than by the communities they described, most imperial archives treat certain voices as documents and others as absences. The seamlessness of such a collection is not a sign of thoroughness; it is a sign of whose perspective was treated as default.

Granted, a collection riddled with holes is harder to work with than a well-ordered one. Historians, legal researchers, and community organisations that depend on archived materials to make claims — to prove land ownership, to seek reparations, to trace family histories — suffer real consequences when records have been destroyed or were never kept. Incompleteness is not always a philosophical virtue; sometimes it is simply an injustice.

Even so, there is a difference between loss that is acknowledged and loss that is hidden behind an appearance of completeness. A collection that marks its own gaps — that notates 'record destroyed', 'community not consulted', 'oral testimony excluded' — is epistemically more honest than one that presents itself as comprehensive without ever disclosing its selection criteria. The acknowledged lacuna tells the researcher something true. The false wholeness tells them a story designed to look like the whole truth.

In recent decades, counter-archive projects in South Africa, Australia, and across Latin America have demonstrated that communities excluded from official records are capable of building their own. These projects are not comprehensive either — they preserve what survived, which is uneven and partial. But they are transparent about that partiality in a way that state archives rarely are. Their incompleteness is honest, and honest incompleteness is more useful to anyone who wants to understand the past than a polished collection that hides the violence of its own curation.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Điều mà một khoảng trống trong kho lưu trữ ghi lại không phải là sự vắng mặt mà là hình dạng của một quyết định

sampleAnnotations.phrase: a decision about whose experience was considered worth preserving and whose could be discarded without consequence
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: một quyết định về trải nghiệm của ai được coi là đáng bảo tồn và của ai có thể bị loại bỏ mà không có hậu quả

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the resulting lacunae in the official record were not accidental losses. They were acts
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: những khoảng trống kết quả trong hồ sơ chính thức không phải là những mất mát ngẫu nhiên. Chúng là những hành động

sampleAnnotations.phrase: an archivist working with those gaps, knowing what was destroyed and why, learns more from them than from any seamlessly complete collection

sampleAnnotations.phrase: The pursuit of completeness in archives is often presented as a neutral, professional ideal

sampleAnnotations.phrase: In practice, it is anything but

sampleAnnotations.phrase: Having been assembled by colonial administrators rather than by the communities they described
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Đã được lắp ráp bởi các nhà quản lý thuộc địa chứ không phải bởi các cộng đồng mà chúng mô tả

sampleAnnotations.phrase: most imperial archives treat certain voices as documents and others as absences
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: hầu hết các kho lưu trữ đế quốc coi một số tiếng nói là tài liệu và những tiếng nói khác là sự vắng mặt

sampleAnnotations.phrase: The seamlessness of such a collection is not a sign of thoroughness; it is a sign of whose perspective was treated as default

sampleAnnotations.phrase: Incompleteness is not always a philosophical virtue; sometimes it is simply an injustice

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to redefine what a gap actually records
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a gap in an archive records is not absence but the shape of a decision' dùng 'What X records is not Y but Z' để bác bỏ cách hiểu thông thường ('absence') và thay thế bằng cách hiểu của tác giả ('the shape of a decision').
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a gap in an archive records is not absence but the shape of a decision' uses a what-cleft to contest the obvious reading of a gap (simple absence) and substitute the essay's central claim: that gaps are decisions, not accidents.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive participle clause to foreground the origin of bias
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Having been assembled by colonial administrators rather than by the communities they described' dùng bị động + mệnh đề phân từ hoàn thành để nhấn mạnh ai đã không tham gia vào việc tạo ra kho lưu trữ, trước khi mệnh đề chính nêu hậu quả.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Having been assembled by colonial administrators rather than by the communities they described' uses a passive perfect participle clause to foreground the exclusion of the described communities from the archive's construction — establishing bias before the main clause names its consequence.

sampleTranslationVi:
Điều mà một khoảng trống trong kho lưu trữ ghi lại không phải là sự vắng mặt mà là hình dạng của một quyết định — một quyết định về trải nghiệm của ai được coi là đáng bảo tồn và của ai có thể bị loại bỏ mà không có hậu quả. Khi chính quyền thuộc địa Anh của Kenya tiêu hủy hàng nghìn tài liệu trước khi độc lập năm 1963, những khoảng trống kết quả trong hồ sơ chính thức không phải là những mất mát ngẫu nhiên. Chúng là những hành động. Và một người lưu trữ làm việc với những khoảng trống đó, biết những gì đã bị phá hủy và tại sao, học được nhiều hơn từ chúng so với bất kỳ bộ sưu tập hoàn chỉnh liền mạch nào.

Việc theo đuổi sự đầy đủ trong kho lưu trữ thường được trình bày như một lý tưởng trung lập, chuyên nghiệp. Trong thực tế, nó hoàn toàn không phải vậy. Mọi kho lưu trữ đều là sản phẩm của các lựa chọn: phải thu thập gì, phân loại gì, lưu trữ gì, và — quan trọng nhất — phải bỏ qua gì. Đã được lắp ráp bởi các nhà quản lý thuộc địa chứ không phải bởi các cộng đồng mà chúng mô tả, hầu hết các kho lưu trữ đế quốc coi một số tiếng nói là tài liệu và những tiếng nói khác là sự vắng mặt. Sự liền mạch của một bộ sưu tập như vậy không phải là dấu hiệu của sự kỹ lưỡng; đó là dấu hiệu của quan điểm của ai được coi là mặc định.

Thừa nhận, một bộ sưu tập đầy lỗ hổng khó làm việc hơn một bộ sưu tập được sắp xếp tốt. Các nhà sử học, nhà nghiên cứu pháp lý và các tổ chức cộng đồng phụ thuộc vào tài liệu lưu trữ để đưa ra các yêu cầu — chứng minh quyền sở hữu đất, tìm kiếm bồi thường, truy tìm lịch sử gia đình — phải chịu hậu quả thực sự khi hồ sơ đã bị phá hủy hoặc chưa bao giờ được lưu giữ. Sự không đầy đủ không phải lúc nào cũng là một đức tính triết học; đôi khi nó chỉ đơn giản là bất công.

Dẫu vậy, có một sự khác biệt giữa mất mát được thừa nhận và mất mát bị ẩn giấu sau vẻ ngoài của sự đầy đủ. Một bộ sưu tập đánh dấu những khoảng trống của chính nó — ghi chú 'hồ sơ bị phá hủy', 'cộng đồng không được tham khảo', 'lời khai truyền miệng bị loại trừ' — trung thực hơn về mặt nhận thức luận so với một bộ sưu tập tự trình bày là toàn diện mà không bao giờ tiết lộ tiêu chí lựa chọn của mình. Khoảng trống được thừa nhận nói với nhà nghiên cứu điều gì đó đúng. Sự toàn vẹn giả dối kể cho họ một câu chuyện được thiết kế để trông như toàn bộ sự thật.

Trong những thập kỷ gần đây, các dự án kho lưu trữ phản kháng ở Nam Phi, Úc và khắp Châu Mỹ Latinh đã chứng minh rằng các cộng đồng bị loại khỏi hồ sơ chính thức có khả năng xây dựng hồ sơ của riêng họ. Các dự án này cũng không toàn diện — chúng bảo tồn những gì còn sống sót, điều này không đều và không đầy đủ. Nhưng chúng minh bạch về sự không đầy đủ đó theo cách mà các kho lưu trữ nhà nước hiếm khi thể hiện. Sự không đầy đủ của chúng là trung thực, và sự không đầy đủ trung thực hữu ích hơn cho bất kỳ ai muốn hiểu quá khứ so với một bộ sưu tập được đánh bóng ẩn giấu bạo lực của chính quá trình chọn lọc của nó.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to redefine what an archival gap actually records?
options: What a gap in an archive records is not absence but the shape of a decision.
Archive gaps are not absence but decisions shaped by what records them.
It is the absence that a gap in an archive records as a shaped decision.
The shape of a decision is what absence records in archive gaps.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What X records is not Y but Z' contests the obvious reading (gaps = absence) and replaces it with the essay's claim (gaps = decisions).
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … records is not … but …' with the writer's reframing at the end.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a passive perfect participle clause to highlight excluded perspectives?
options: Having been assembled by colonial administrators rather than by the communities they described, most imperial archives treat certain voices as documents and others as absences.
Most imperial archives assembled colonial administrators described communities voices documents absences.
Having assembled colonial administrators most imperial archives treat voices as described communities.
Communities described having assembled colonial administrators imperial archives voices absences.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Having been assembled by X rather than by Y' is a passive perfect participle clause; its implied subject (archives) matches the main clause subject.
mcQuestions.hint: The passive form 'having been assembled' signals who was — and who was not — involved.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'lacuna' mean in the context of archives?
options: A large storage room for physical documents
A gap, missing section, or absence in a collection or record
A formal index of archived materials
A legal document authorising access to classified records
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Lacuna' (plural: lacunae) is a formal term for a gap or missing element in a text, record, or collection.
mcQuestions.hint: The word is used in the essay alongside 'gaps' and 'holes' — what do they all have in common?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's main argument about acknowledged gaps versus false completeness?
options: Acknowledged gaps are a sign of poor record-keeping that should be corrected.
A collection that marks its own gaps is epistemically more honest than one presenting false completeness.
Archives should always aim for completeness, even if it means fabricating missing records.
Gaps only matter in colonial archives; other archives are reliably complete.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues: 'A collection that marks its own gaps is epistemically more honest than one that presents itself as comprehensive without disclosing its selection criteria.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the paragraph comparing 'acknowledged lacuna' with 'false wholeness'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which concession does the essay make about incomplete archives?
options: Admitted, incomplete archives are always better than complete ones.
Granted, a collection riddled with holes is harder to work with than a well-ordered one.
Granted, incompleteness is a purely philosophical virtue with no practical value.
Admittedly, only colonial archives suffer from meaningful gaps.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay concedes that gaps create real practical difficulties for researchers and communities — before pivoting to distinguish acknowledged gaps from hidden ones.
mcQuestions.hint: Find the sentence that admits the opposing view honestly before 'Even so, …'

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to curate' mean in the context of an archive?
options: To physically repair damaged documents using conservation techniques
To select, organise, and present materials according to a set of criteria
To digitise a collection and make it available online
To return archived materials to their original owners
mcQuestions.explanation: 'To curate' means to select and organise with deliberate judgment — in archives, this involves deciding what is worth keeping and how it should be presented.
mcQuestions.hint: A museum curator selects which objects appear in an exhibition — how does that transfer to archives?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'selection bias' mean in the context of archiving?
options: The tendency of archives to prefer older documents over newer ones
A systematic skew in what is preserved because certain things are consistently chosen over others
The legal requirement to preserve only materials requested by governments
The preference of archivists for documents written in dominant languages
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Selection bias' means that the collection does not represent the full range of what existed because the selection process was skewed toward certain types, voices, or perspectives.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option describes a systematic, not random, skew in what ends up being preserved?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction correctly focuses on the act of suppression rather than on its agent?
options: Colonial administrators suppressed thousands of documents before independence.
Thousands of documents were suppressed before independence, leaving gaps in the official record.
The official record suppressed thousands documents colonial administrators before independence.
Before independence thousands suppressed documents colonial administrators record official.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Were suppressed' uses the passive to foreground the documents and the resulting gaps rather than the administrators — the effect on the record is what matters.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option puts the documents in subject position without naming the agent?

options: Written in a clearer and more readable style
More financially transparent in its accounting practices
More truthful about what it knows, does not know, and how it came to know it
More frequently audited by an independent body
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Epistemic' relates to knowledge — 'epistemically honest' means being truthful about the sources, limits, and conditions of what is known.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Episteme' comes from the Greek for knowledge — what does it mean to be honest about that?

options: It destroys official records to prevent them from being misused
It provides a parallel collection documenting what official archives excluded or suppressed
It stores backup copies of government records in a secure location
It formally challenges the accuracy of official records in court
mcQuestions.explanation: A counter-archive is built by communities or researchers to document experiences, voices, and events that official archives ignored or actively suppressed.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Counter' means 'against' or 'alternative' — what would an archive built against the official one contain?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the phrase 'In practice, it is anything but' mean?
options: In practice, it turns out to be exactly what it appears to be
In practice, the opposite is true — it is far from neutral or simple
In practice, it remains poorly understood by practitioners
In practice, it is rarely attempted despite its theoretical appeal
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Anything but X' means 'the complete opposite of X' — the essay is saying that archive completeness is not neutral at all, despite being presented as such.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Anything but simple' means 'very complicated' — apply the same logic here.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'transparent' correctly in an epistemic context?
options: These projects are transparent about their partiality in a way that state archives rarely are.
State archives are transparent partiality rarely projects about these way.
Transparent these projects rarely are partiality state archives about way.
About partiality these transparent projects state archives rarely in a way are.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Transparent about X' means openly acknowledging X rather than concealing it — here, the counter-archives admit their limitations openly.
mcQuestions.hint: Subject + 'are transparent about' + noun phrase is the correct structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'provenance' mean in the context of archived documents?
options: The physical condition of a document after years of storage
The origin, history of ownership, and chain of custody of a document
The translation of a document from one language to another
The legal copyright status of a document
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Provenance' means where something comes from and who has held it — in archiving, knowing a document's provenance is essential for assessing its reliability.
mcQuestions.hint: The word comes from French 'provenir' — to come from. What does knowing where something 'comes from' tell you?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option maintains formal register throughout?
options: Old archives are honestly kind of unreliable and people who use them should know that.
It's pretty obvious that some archives are missing stuff because of colonialism and that's bad.
The acknowledged lacuna tells the researcher something true. The false wholeness tells them a story designed to look like the whole truth.
Archives with holes are actually better than perfect ones because at least you know what's missing right.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses precise academic vocabulary ('lacuna', 'false wholeness') in complete, formal syntactic structures with no informal fillers.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'honestly', 'pretty obvious', 'right?', and other informal hedges.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what did the British colonial administration of Kenya do before independence in 1963?
options: They published a comprehensive catalogue of all official records
They handed all official records over to the incoming Kenyan government
They destroyed thousands of documents, creating deliberate gaps in the official record
They sealed their archives and transferred them to the UK for safekeeping
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states: 'When the British colonial administration of Kenya destroyed thousands of documents before independence in 1963, the resulting lacunae in the official record were not accidental losses. They were acts.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the opening paragraph — what specific action is described?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is 'epistemic violence' as implied in the essay?
options: Physical destruction of library buildings during armed conflict
The harm caused by systematically erasing, suppressing, or misrepresenting the knowledge and experiences of a group
The aggressive academic debate over whose version of history is correct
A criminal act of stealing rare documents from an archive
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Epistemic violence' refers to the harm done when the knowledge, voice, or way of knowing of a group is suppressed, dismissed, or erased — often by more powerful institutions.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Episteme' = knowledge; what kind of 'violence' would target that?

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is there a story from your family, community, or national history that was never officially recorded? What would an honest gap marker say about why it was left out, and who would benefit from acknowledging it?
